Committee To Elect Judge Burleigh                                                                Steve Magyar
                                                                                                                          Campaign Chairman
30 Via Encanto
Monterey, CA 93940                                                                                              Anne Baggiolini
                                                                                                                          Campaign Coordinator
Kenneth B. Petersen, Treasurer                                                                            Mary Miller
                                                                                                                          Campaign Manager


Committee                                                                                    4/25/90

Julia Alsop
Herb Blanks
Willard Branson
Al Brillantes
Richard Brinton
Meg Conners
Jack Davenport
Ted Durein
Richard Eldred
Lilyan Eldred
William Ellis
Willis "Bill" Haines
Russ Harris
Ben Heinrich
Clyde Klaumann
Barney Laiolo
William R. Lewis, M.D.
Gwen Manzoni
Tom Maudlin
Alice Moe
Michael Panelli
Marshall Rouhala
George Takahashi
William H. Temple

Judge William Burleigh Announces
Candidacy for Superior Court


January 29, 1990

Salinas --

     Judge William Burleigh of the Monterey County Municipal

Court has announced that he is seeking election to the Superior

Court seat held by Judge Richard Silver.

     "I feel," said Burleigh, "that after 17 years on the

Municipal Court bench, it is time to apply my experience to

the challenges of a higher court.  I have chosen to run against

Judge Silver because I am disturbed by his decisions over the

past several years.  Judge Silver is an activist, take-charge

judge, appointed by Gov. Jerry Brown.  His decisions show a

consistent pattern of judicial interference with business,

government and individuals.

     "Judges should leave law making to the legislators, and

law enforcement to the executive branch," said Burleigh.  "I

believe strongly in the constitutional separation of powers."

Attached is a list of some of the cases that Burleigh feels

illustrate Judge Silver's activism.

     "I intend to give the voters a clear choice between an

activist, liberal judge and a conservative, no-nonsense

judge," said Burleigh.

     Judge Burleigh's qualifications to serve as a Superior

Court Judge include:


1

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

1.  Seventeen years as a Municipal Court Judge, in both Monterey
    and Salinas, handling thousands of criminal and civil cases.

2.  Faculty, California Judge's College.

3.  Author of numerous articles and columns in legal
    publications.

4.  Vice-President, California Judge's Association.

5.  Associate Editor, California Court's Commentary (Judges'
    law journal), 1976 to present.

                    PERSONAL BACKGROUND

1.  Age, 55.  Married 29 years, three adult daughters.

2.  Education:  B.A., University of Colorado (Cum Laude);
    J.D., University of California, Berkeley (Boalt Hall),
    Moot Court Board.

3.  Military:  Served to Sergeant, U.S. Marine Corps.

                    LEGAL EXPERIENCE

1.  Private general practice for nine years, with emphasis
    on trials.

2.  City Attorney of Carmel for eight years; named City Attorney
    of the Year for California, 1972.

3.  Vice-President, Monterey County Bar Association.

4.  Lecturer, Continuing Education of the Bar.

                    COMMUNITY INVOLVEMENT

1.  Founder of two charity fundraisers (Big Sur Marathon, Big
    Sur River Run) that have raised one-quarter of a million
    dollars for needy Monterey County agencies.

2.  Has served as a director of seven community organizations,
    including Jaycees, Rotary, Parent/Faculty Board and Legal
    Aid Society.

3.  Boy Scout Counselor.


                                2

Cases Decided by Judge Richard Silver

1.    Safeway Store in Marina, faced with competition from the
new Lucky Store, announced that it was shutting down because
it was losing $20,000 a month and could not find a
substitute tenant.  Although they agreed to keep paying
rent, the owner of the shopping center sued Safeway because
the loss of the major tenant would hurt the smaller stores
in the center.  Judge Silver ordered Safeway to remain
open.
                          Mitchell v. Safeway, No. 19056

2.    A peace officer was told that the defendant, who the officer
knew was a convicted murderer on parole, was selling drugs
out of the back of his pickup camper.  When confronted
by peace officers he openly attempted to hide the drugs,
and the officers seized them.  Judge Silver dismissed the
case because the defendant's rights were violated.  (He
was later reversed by the Appellate Court.)
                  People v. William Andrus (Cromier), No. 6152

3.    For over seven years Judge Silver refused to permit the
Carmel Valley Master Plan to take effect because it did
not meet his standards.
                     Carmel v. Board of Supervisors, No. 75918

4.    Two teenage girls wanted to go to the prom but their boy-
friends did not.  The Student Council denied them tickets,
and the superintendent and the adult School Board agreed.
Judge Silver ordered the school to give them tickets.
                  Hawkins v. Salinas Un. High School, No. 83710

5.    The defendant and the victim got into a fight in a parking
lot.  The defendant was losing badly and left the scene,
returned shortly with a knife, stabbed the victim and
them chased him out to the street and killed him.  The
fight was over twice before the defendant stabbed him the
last time.  Judge Silver said there was no malice and found
the defendant guilty of manslaughter instead of second
degree murder.
                         People v. Gregory Green, No. 6447

6.    A high school student was given home study for nine weeks
for having beer on his breath in the school parking lot.
Judge Silver reversed the school principal, school superin-
tendent, and elected School Board, and ordered the student
reinstated in school.
                            Hufford v. Agee, No. 19600

7.    Judge Silver ruled that it is permissible for the Federal
Government to set the speed limit on the Carmel Valley
Road.  And he would not certify (refer) the case to a higher
court.
                         People v. Williams, No. CR 11142


                                    3
